Triggers.value: When people say things like my ex

Maladaptive Strategies.value: Situations in which other people evaluate me / my appearance / my performance

Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.
I can talk about what happened very easily.

Negative Appraisals.value: I will never be able to feel normal emotions again
I can't stop bad things from happening to me
I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.

vignette:
She is a 28-year-old woman from Oceania, currently in a relationship, who presents with prominent post-traumatic symptoms following an accident. Her PCL-5 score of 60 indicates a substantial level of distress and functional impact. In day-to-day life she describes moments in which a voice, a news item, or even a passing remark such as “my ex” can pull her abruptly back into the event. These reminders are followed by a surge in distress, with sudden flashbacks in which she feels or acts as though the frightening experience is happening again, alongside being jumpy, easily startled, and sleeping poorly.

Her memory of the accident is organized in vivid, picture-like snapshots rather than a continuous narrative. She can also speak about what happened with relative ease, yet the images remain clear and detailed, and they seem to sharpen the impact of later reminders. When the event comes to mind, she notices how quickly it can intensify her sense of current danger and how it shapes the meanings she draws about herself and her future. She has come to feel that she will never be able to experience normal emotions again, that bad things cannot be stopped, and that she used to be a happy person but is now always miserable.

These appraisals sit close to the surface in her everyday life and appear to broaden the range of things that feel threatening. They are also tied to the reminders she notices around her, so that external cues gain extra significance and are more readily detected. She describes avoiding going out, looking down to limit eye contact, and staying away from situations in which others might evaluate her appearance or performance. Her sense of being on edge remains high, and the startle response and sleep disruption keep her depleted.

Although she remains in a relationship, her avoidance narrows shared activities and leaves her increasingly isolated. The combination of vivid recollections, persistent threat, and repeated efforts to keep herself from being exposed to reminders appears to maintain the cycle of distress. Her presentation is consistent with a pattern in which reminders, intrusive memory, threat, and negative appraisals continually reinforce one another, making recovery feel distant and everyday functioning increasingly restricted.
